title: Protected Areas in News - Overview
focus: Geographical mapping and awareness of recently highlighted protected areas in India.
rationale: Tests map-based knowledge and basic awareness of various protected areas that were recently in the news.
sectionExcerpt: List of Protected Areas highlighted on the map of India: Bhagirathi Eco Sensitive Zone (ESZ), Tsarap Chu Conservation Reserve, Dudhwa Tiger Reserve (DTR), Salkhan Fossil Park, Kaziranga National Park and Tiger Reserve, Keibul Lamjao National Park, Sundarbans Tiger Reserve (STR), Similipal Tiger Reserve, Mahuadanr Wolf Sanctuary, Male Mahadeshwara (MM) Hills Wildlife Sanctuary, Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve, Thattekad Bird Sanctuary, Mhadei Wildlife Sanctuary, Sahyadri Tiger Reserve.
evidenceBullets: Bhagirathi Eco Sensitive Zone (ESZ)
Tsarap Chu Conservation Reserve
Dudhwa Tiger Reserve (DTR)
Salkhan Fossil Park
Kaziranga National Park and Tiger Reserve
Keibul Lamjao National Park
Sundarbans Tiger Reserve (STR)
Similipal Tiger Reserve

title: Kaziranga National Park and Tiger Reserve (Assam)
focus: Ecological features, fauna, river systems, and recent census techniques at Kaziranga.
rationale: Kaziranga is a highly examinable topic for UPSC due to its unique fauna (Rhino, Tiger density), geography, and recent use of Acoustic Monitoring.
sectionExcerpt: First-ever grassland bird census was held here using Acoustic Monitoring Technique. Location: Brahmaputra floodplain, at foothills of Karbi-Anglong. River: Diffalu and Moradifalu. Forest type: Alluvial grasslands, semi-evergreen and moist deciduous forests, wetlands, and sandy chaurs. Key Fauna: One-Horned Rhino (70% of population), Tiger, Eastern swamp deer, Elephant, Hoolock gibbon, Capped langur and Gangetic River Dolphin etc. Recorded 3rd highest tiger density in India after Karnataka's Bandipur and Uttarakhand's Corbett National Park. Recognized as an Important Bird Area by BirdLife International.
evidenceBullets: First-ever grassland bird census was conducted using Acoustic Monitoring Technique.
Located on the Brahmaputra floodplain, at the foothills of Karbi-Anglong.
Diffalu and Moradifalu rivers flow through the park.
Hosts 70% of the One-Horned Rhino population.
Records the 3rd highest tiger density in India after Bandipur and Corbett.
Recognized as an Important Bird Area by BirdLife International.

title: Mhadei Wildlife Sanctuary (Goa)
focus: Geography, flora, and fauna of Mhadei Wildlife Sanctuary.
rationale: Important due to the Supreme Court stay on projects and its strategic location in the Western Ghats.
sectionExcerpt: Context: Recently, Supreme Court stayed projects here. Location: North Goa along Western Ghats, bordering Karnataka and Maharashtra. River: Mahadei (Mondovi) River flows through wildlife sanctuary. Fauna: Tigers, leopards, sloth bears, gaurs, sambar deer, etc. Forest type: Moist deciduous vegetation and some evergreen species, e.g. rare Ashoka tree with peculiar saffron colored flowers.
evidenceBullets: Located in North Goa along the Western Ghats, bordering Karnataka and Maharashtra.
Mahadei (Mondovi) River flows through the sanctuary.
Features moist deciduous and evergreen vegetation, including the rare Ashoka tree with saffron-colored flowers.

title: Tsarap Chu Conservation Reserve (Himachal Pradesh)
focus: Location, boundaries, and unique alpine fauna of India's largest conservation reserve.
rationale: As India's largest conservation reserve, recently notified, with significant high-altitude fauna, it is a prime candidate for environment questions.
sectionExcerpt: Himachal Pradesh notified it (India's largest conservation reserve) in Spiti Valley. Location: Confluence of Unam River and Charap Nala. Bounded by UT of Ladakh to north, the Kibber Wildlife Sanctuary extending up to Malang Nala and Lungar Lungpa to east, Kabjima Nala to south and Chandratal Wildlife Sanctuary to west. Fauna: Known for snow leopard, Tibetan wolf, bharal (blue sheep), Himalayan ibex, kiang (wild ass), and Tibetan argali, rare bird species such as Rose Finch, Tibetan Raven, etc.
evidenceBullets: India's largest conservation reserve, located in Spiti Valley, Himachal Pradesh.
Situated at the confluence of the Unam River and Charap Nala.
Bounded by Ladakh to the north, Kibber WLS to the east, and Chandratal WLS to the west.
Fauna includes snow leopard, Tibetan wolf, bharal (blue sheep), Himalayan ibex, kiang (wild ass), and Tibetan argali.

title: Mahuadanr Wolf Sanctuary (Jharkhand)
focus: Unique cultural practices and ecological significance of India's only wolf sanctuary.
rationale: India's first and only dedicated wolf sanctuary; involves unique tribal conservation practices which makes it highly relevant for UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: Witnessed unique practice of tribals from the 'Sarna faith' to avoid the Sal Forest during winter months. Location: India's first and only dedicated wolf sanctuary, located within the Palamau Tiger Reserve in Jharkhand. River: Burha River flow through Mahuadanr valley.
evidenceBullets: India's first and only dedicated wolf sanctuary.
Located within the Palamau Tiger Reserve in Jharkhand.
Tribals from the 'Sarna faith' practice avoiding the Sal Forest during winter months.
Burha River flows through the Mahuadanr valley.

title: Male Mahadeshwara (MM) Hills Wildlife Sanctuary (Karnataka)
focus: Ecological corridor role and forest types of MM Hills Wildlife Sanctuary.
rationale: Crucial ecological corridor connecting the Eastern and Western Ghats, making it significant for landscape-level conservation questions.
sectionExcerpt: Location: Southeast Karnataka. Forms connecting corridor between Biligiri Ranganatha Swamy Temple (BRT) Wildlife Sanctuary and Cauvery Wildlife Sanctuary. Forest type: Dominated by dry deciduous forests and Patches of Semi-evergreen, Shola forests at higher altitudes and scrub forests in fringe zones. Fauna: Tiger, Elephant, Leopard, Wild dog (dhole), Sloth bear, etc. Important corridor in the Eastern Ghats-Western Ghats landscape.
evidenceBullets: Located in Southeast Karnataka.
Forms a connecting corridor between BRT Wildlife Sanctuary and Cauvery Wildlife Sanctuary.
Serves as an important corridor in the Eastern Ghats-Western Ghats landscape.
Features dry deciduous forests, semi-evergreen patches, and Shola forests at higher altitudes.
